coredata.dc:title: A theoretical study of the functionalized carbon dots surfaces binding with silver nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Robein, Y.
Ambrusi, R.E.
Pronsato, M.E.
Di Nezio, M.S.
Brizuela, G.
coredata.dc:description: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out to evaluate the interaction of silver nanostructures on non-functionalized and functionalized carbon dots (Cdots) surfaces. Coronene and coronene with an epoxy functional group are used to model the Cdots surfaces and small size clusters of Agn (n = 1.0.3) are employed to represent the silver nanostructures. A theoretical study of the stability and structure of the composite Ag@Cdots material is performed. The essential role of oxygenated functional groups of Cdots functionalized surfaces to attach silver clusters is systematically evaluated with focus on the understanding of the mechanism intervening on the Cdots surfaces and silver nanostructures interaction. The electronic structure and charge distribution are analyzed. Additionally a bonding investigation is also developed as an attempt to characterize the most relevant bonds contributing to anchor silver nanostructures on Cdots surfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Silver nanostructures
DFT
Binding energy
Hybrid nanoparticles

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank to ANPCyT (PICT-2016-4094). R.E. Ambrusi, M.E. Pronsato, and G. Brizuela are members of CONICET. G. Brizuela acknowledges SGCyT, UNS, Argentina (PGI 24/F078). M.S. Di Nezio acknowledges SGCyT, UNS, Argentina (PGI 24/Q110) and INQUISUR (UNS-CONICET).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structures for the substrates; A) Coronene and B) Coronene-Epoxi.

Optimized structures for the Agn (n = 1–3) clusters adsorbed on Coronene and Coronene-Epoxy substrates; A) Ag1/Coronene, B) Ag2/Coronene, C) Ag3/Coronene, D) Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi, E) Ag2/Coronene-Epoxi and F) Ag3/Coronene-Epoxi.
DOS curves of A) Ag1 isolated cluster, B) isolated Coronene and Ag1/Coronene and C) projections on C and Ag atoms of Ag1/Coronene.
DOS curves of A) Ag1 isolated cluster, B) isolated Coronene-Epoxi and Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi and C) projections on O and Ag atoms of Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi.
Bader charges represented by colors of A) Coronene, B) Coronene-Epoxi, C) Ag1/Coronene and D) Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi. Darker atoms have higher net charges.

Isosurfaces of the charge density differences for A) Ag1/Coronene, B) Ag2/Coronene, C) Ag3/Coronene and D) Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi, E) Ag2/Coronene-Epoxi and Ag3/Coronene-Epoxi. The red and blue colors correspond to the charge depletion and accumulation regions respectively, for a value for the isosurfaces of 0.01 e/Å3. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Selected atoms for the OP and BO analysis of the systems A) Ag1/Coronene, B) Ag2/Coronene, C) Ag3/Coronene, D) Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi, E) Ag2/Coronene-Epoxi and Ag3/Coronene-Epoxi.
Binding energies of the Agn cluster on coronene non-functionalized and functionalized with epoxy group, and distances between Ag atom and closer coronene atom.
Average Bader Charges for C, O, H and Ag and charge transfer between cluster and support for the optimized Coronene and Coronene-Epoxi with and without silver clusters adsorbed.
OP, BO and bond distances of optimized Agn/Coronene systems.
OP, BO and bond distances of optimized Agn/Coronene-Epoxi systems.

head.ce:title: A theoretical study of the functionalized carbon dots surfaces binding with silver nanostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Física del Sur (IFISUR), CONICET, Av. L. N. Alem 1253, B8000CPB-Bahía Blanca, Argentina

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Química, Universidad Nacional del Sur (UNS), Av. L. N. Alem 1253, B8000CPB-Bahía Blanca, Argentina
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departamento de Química, Universidad Nacional del Sur (UNS), Av. L. N. Alem 1253, B8000CPB-Bahía Blanca, Argentina

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Departamento de Física, Universidad Nacional del Sur, Av. Alem 1253, 8000 Bahía Blanca, Argentina.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were carried out to evaluate the interaction of silver nanostructures on non-functionalized and functionalized carbon dots (Cdots) surfaces. Coronene and coronene with an epoxy functional group are used to model the Cdots surfaces and small size clusters of Agn (n = 1.0.3) are employed to represent the silver nanostructures. A theoretical study of the stability and structure of the composite Ag@Cdots material is performed. The essential role of oxygenated functional groups of Cdots functionalized surfaces to attach silver clusters is systematically evaluated with focus on the understanding of the mechanism intervening on the Cdots surfaces and silver nanostructures interaction. The electronic structure and charge distribution are analyzed. Additionally a bonding investigation is also developed as an attempt to characterize the most relevant bonds contributing to anchor silver nanostructures on Cdots surfaces.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanomaterials has become of huge interest for researchers because of their many desirable properties. Owing to their small size such nanoscale materials have physical, electronic and chemical properties that are different from those of bulk counterparts [1]. Such properties strongly depend on the nature and size of the nanostructures under consideration. For instance, silver nanoparticles have attracted a lot of attention on one hand because of their optical properties [2,3]. On the other hand, silver nanoparticles have been used to modify different carbon paste electrodes in order to detect Cd and Pb ions by voltammetric technique [4]. This kind of composite material was also obtained using glassy carbon electrodes [5,6]. Additionally, carbon nanoparticles, also known as Cdots, were used to modify glassy carbon electrodes and used for heavy metal detection through square wave anodic stripping voltammetry [7]. This type of process can be favored by the under potential deposition (UPD) of these metals [6]. For that reason, it is an advantage to use silver nanoparticles because Cd and Pb ions have UPD over Ag surfaces [6,8–11]. Consequently, it is worth using the modification of Cdots with silver crystals to exploit the properties of the formed composed material in the detection of Cd and Pb metals.

Experimental studies have shown different other uses of silver nanostructures. For instance, previous investigations [12,13] show that silver mesowires prepared by electrochemical deposition on graphitic electrodes, can be applied for amine sensing. Additionally, silver electrodes have potential properties as catalyst. In this sense, Xing et al. [14] describe that silver single crystal presents electrocatalytic properties for the nitrate reduction, and this effect can be enhanced considerable by the presence of Cd ions. Moreover, in a previous work Ambrusi et al. [15] have taken advantage of this last characteristic studying the electrocatalytic effect of Cd-Ag bimetallic nanoparticles supported on graphite and glassy carbon substrates for the nitrate and/or nitrite ions reduction reaction. For that reason, the study of silver nanoparticles modifying Cdots constitute an excellent platform for potential applications in different fields. In addition, the fabrication of Cdots supported on glassy carbon electrodes [7], establishes a solid start point for silver modified Cdots potential applications on the fields described previously.

C-dots are basically carbon nanoparticles with size below 10 nm [16], that are synthesized in multiple ways including top-down approaches as arc discharge, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, or bottom-up approaches like combustion, template, hydrothermal, ultrasonic, microwave and solvothermal processes [17]. In a recent publication, Arroyave et al. [18] proposed a novel synthesis of Cdots and Silver/Carbon hybrid nanoparticles employing glucose as precursor and capping agent with a microwave exposure time between 30 and 60 s. Also, the electronic and optical properties of Cdots were studied both theoretically and experimentally [16–22]. Furthermore, Ambrusi et al. [23] modeled Cdots employing simple polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons structures. A similar model was also used to characterize the interactions between silver and Cdots, explaining also experimental infrared spectroscopic features [24]. In addition, an experimental and theoretical approach was performed in order to characterize the structure of Cdots proposing a carbon core cover by polymer structures conformed by carboxylic acid and aldehyde monomers [25].

Recent theoretical studies were performed by Jadoon et al. [26–31] analyzing the interaction between Agn clusters and coronene and their application for the detection of gaseous analytes, as electrochemical sensor of glucose, H2O2, trinitrotoluene, p-nitrophenol and nitroaniline isomers and adsorption mechanism of p-Aminophenol. This type of approach was performed also to model Cdots or more specifically graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [32]. GQDs are graphene structures with finite size [19,23], and as graphene, they consist of a single atomic layer of graphite. A common way to model GQDs in theoretical works is using a coronene molecule [20,25,32]. Instead, Cdots have a more complex nature which makes the understanding of the physical and chemical properties very challenging [33]. However, there is evidence that these carbon spheres can have a graphitic structure in their carbon core [34,35], constituted by many graphene layers interacting only physically with each other. For that reason, they could be modeled by a single C layer since the layer on the surface only interacts physically with the next, without affecting the electronic structure. Hence, even though GQDs are not strictly the same as Cdots, from a theoretical point of view they become a suitable model of the surface of Cdots. For this reason, using a GQD modeled by coronene constitutes an appropriate chemical and physical environment to study the interactions between Cdot surfaces and metal nanostructures. This is reinforced by the fact that, as demonstrated by Jadoon et al. [27,28], an ultraviolet visible band at 262 nm is predicted, in a high level of accordance with experimental measurements of Cdots (265 nm) [18]. The functionalization of the carbon rings plane of coronene to model the Cdots surface has not been performed yet, and is important because the Cdots surfaces contain functional groups [33], and also, these groups can enhance the binding between silver and Cdots [23,24], and consequently change physical chemistry characteristics of the system.

The aim of the present work is to study the interaction of Ag clusters and Cdots surfaces modeled as functionalized and non-functionalized coronene using DFT calculations. The charge distribution, electronics structure and a bond analysis were performed in order to develop a systematic investigation of hybrid Ag@Cdots nanoparticles formed from the adsorption of silver clusters on Cdot surfaces. Additionally, the role of epoxy functional group to anchor the Agn cluster will be analyzed, which up to our knowledge has not been previously performed. This kind of study generates also a solid framework for the subsequent theoretical characterization of Ag@Cdots nanoparticles for the potential application in the detection of analytes like Cd and Pb metal ions.

DFT calculations were performed using the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) [36,37], which employs a plane-wave basis set and a periodic supercell method. The generalized gradient corrected approximation (GGA) functional of Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) was employed [38]. The projector- augmented-wave (PAW) method [39,40] was applied to solve variationally the Kohn–Sham equations. A cutoff energy of 500 eV was used for the plane-wave basis set expansion, overall with the gamma-centered Monkhorst–Pack scheme [41] with 1x1x1 k-points grid for the integration over the Brillouin zone. A Gaussian smearing approach was set for the electronic states partial occupations near the Fermi level, with a 0.05 eV smearing width.

Geometry optimizations were carried out by minimizing the total energy of the supercell using a conjugated gradient algorithm to relax ions [42], until it converged within 10-6 eV and the forces on each ion were less than 0.01 eV °A-1. The Grimme's DFT-D2 method was adopted to account for the van der Waals interactions (vdW), which is optimized for several DFT functional [43].

Isolated coronene structures with and without an epoxy group, used to model Cdots surfaces, were optimized in a box of 30x30x30 Å, as performed in previous works following graphene quantum dots model [19,32]. The choice of epoxy group as the only functionalization of Cdot is based on a simplification of the model to perform the calculations. Many experimental works corroborate that oxygen-containing groups exist on the surface of Cdots [18,44–47], such as carboxylic, hydroxyl, carbonyl and ether groups among others. Epoxy group is only an approximation of the effect that these oxygen containing groups can have in the interaction between silver and functionalized Cdots surface, which at the same time is suitable to include on the aromatic rings of coronene without the inclusion of other type of defects. To evaluate the interactions of silver nanostructures and Cdots, the same box cell was used. In order to test the selected model for Ag1/Coronene, it was compared with Ag1/graphene calculations, in a 32 atoms graphene supercell and 4x4x1 K-points mesh. The difference between both models in the silver binding energy and Ag-C distance is 3 and 8 % respectively, having an excellent agreement.

Eqn (1), was used to calculate the binding energy for the silver cluster on the Cdot surface (Ebinding),
(1)
E
binding
= E
Ag
n
+ C d o t
- E
Ag
n
- E
C d o t

where E(Agn + Cdot), E(Agn) and E(Cdot) are the total energies of the Agn cluster adsorbed on Cdot surface (non-functionalized and functionalized), the isolated Agn cluster, and Cdot surface respectively. With this definition negative adsorption energy corresponds to a stable adsorption of the Agn cluster on Cdot surface.

In order to analyze the charge distribution before and after the silver adsorption, the electronic charges on atoms were computed using Bader analysis [48]. Additionally, to complement the charge distribution calculations on the adsorption region, the charge density difference (
Δ ρ
) isosurface was obtained as:
(2)
Δ ρ
=
ρ
Ag
n
/ C d o t
-
ρ
Cdot
-
ρ
Ag
n

In this equation,
ρ
Ag
n
/ C d o t
represents the charge density of the system formed by the Agn cluster adsorbed on the coronene surfaces non-functionalized and functionalized, while
ρ
Cdot
and
ρ
Ag
n
indicate the charge density of the Cdot surfaces without adsorbed Agn cluster and the Agn cluster without surface, respectively.

Density of states (DOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) were obtained at PBE level of theory to study the electronic structure. A qualitative study of the bonding between different atoms was also performed using the overlap population (OP) concept and Bond Order (BO) as implemented in the DDEC6 method [49–51]. This approach is used to compare the bond between pair of atoms for different configurations or chemical environment.

Several theoretical studies have reported the structure, stability, and physical properties of isolated Agn clusters [52–55]. For the optimized geometries, Table 1 shows the values obtained for the binding energy (Eb), the normalized binding energy (Eb/atom) and the average bond lengths corresponding to optimized calculations for the Agn(n = 2–3) ground states. The binding energy was calculated as
E b
=
E
Ag
n
- n
E
Ag
, where
E
Ag
n
is the energy of the isolated Agn cluster,
E
Ag
is the energy of an isolated Ag atom and
n
is the atom number of the cluster. The energy calculations of the clusters were performed in a cubic cell of 30 Å, to avoid the interaction between periodic cell images. The structure relaxation of the clusters was tested with different smearing approaches; Gaussian (G) and Methfessel-Paxton (MP) of order one, since Gaussian smearing is not recommended for metals due to its bad energy estimations. The smearing width (σ) was tested for 0.05 and 0.2 eV to discard influence of these parameters on the geometrical structures and energies.

In Table S1 (Supplementary Material) are reported the most relevant results. The binding energies are practically the same with every method and that Eb/n is slightly lower in absolute value for the higher cluster size. The values of the average bond distance, dAg–Ag, decreased with cluster size reflecting a weaker interaction between Ag atoms as the energy values suggest. The bond distances obtained are in agreement with other theoretical and experimental reports [52–57]. The binding energy of the dimer is in agreement with previous reported theoretical and experimental values [57,58]. From this analysis it is justified the parameters employed to evaluate the Cdots and silver interactions described in the theoretical model section.

In order to evaluate the stability of silver nanocrystals on Cdots surfaces, the Agn clusters are adsorbed on non-functionalized and functionalized surfaces. As previously mentioned, for all the structures; a coronene molecule is used to represent the carbon surface non-functionalized, while a coronene molecule with an epoxy oxygenated functional group is employed to represent the Cdot functionalized surface. These structures are shown in Fig. 1 after geometry optimization. For both supports, different adsorption sites for the silver clusters Agn (n = 1–3) were tested and the energy was minimized, relaxing all the atoms and obtaining the most stable site for each system. The optimized structures are shown in Fig. 2 .

In Fig. 1 it is shown that coronene conserves its shape with a CC and CH bond distance of 1.43 Å and 1.09 Å. respectively. These results are in good agreement with previous works [32,59,60]. The functionalized substrate experiments a slight distortion, with a CC bond distance of approximately 1.46 Å for the ring close to the epoxi group and a CO bond distance of 1.44 Å. The CH bond distance does not change. Fig. 2 shows that the aggregation of silver clusters does not alter the structure of coronene, with the same CC and CH bond distances. Regarding the adsorption of Agn clusters on Coronene-Epoxi substrate, the changes in the structure occur mainly in the functional group, especially the CO bond distance changes between 5 and 9 %. Only the Ag3 cluster causes a deformation of the epoxy group, shifting the O atom from the bridge site (on the bond between two C atoms) to the top site (on top of the C atom).

Table 1, lists the binding energies for the Agn atom cluster (Ebinding) and the bond distance corresponding to a C (O) atom of Coronene (Coronene-Epoxi) and its nearest Ag atom. The binding energy for Ag1 monomer adsorbed on coronene is in high concordance with the silver adatom on graphite surface [57].

Table 1 shows that the binding energies and bond distances between silver and coronene are compatible with a physical interaction when coronene is non-functionalized as was previously reported for silver adsorption on graphite surfaces [57]. Moreover, the bond distance order between silver and carbon are similar to other reports [26,57]. The Agn/Coronene bond distances are higher than 3 Å, which is compatible with a mainly dispersion interaction caused by van der Waals forces. When we evaluate the dispersion energy contribution to the total binding energy, almost 100 % of it corresponds to the dispersion energy confirming this physical interaction. In contrast, this behavior changes when the epoxy group functionalizes the carbon surface leading to a lower dispersion contribution of between 27 and 57 %, and in consequence the interaction has a more important chemical nature. The bond distances between silver and coronene reduce and the binding energy for Ag1 and Ag3 cluster becomes more negative on the functionalized coronene. The Agn/Coronene-Epoxi composite materials have the following order according to stability; Ag3/Coronene-Epoxi > Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi > Ag2/Coronene-Epoxi. However, as mentioned, the Ag3/Coronene-Epoxi system has a distortion of the epoxy functional group.

A study of the electronic structure is performed in order to achieve a better understanding of the Agn clusters binding to coronene (non-functionalized and functionalized). The total density of states (TDOS), and PDOS on C, O and Ag atoms for both substrates with and without adsorbed silver clusters were analyzed and are shown in Figs. 3 and 4
for Ag1 cluster, and Figs. S1-S4 for Agn(n = 2–3) cluster.

In Fig. 3, it can be observed that the states of the isolated Ag1 silver cluster and coronene change in the Ag1/Coronene system. However, there is no clear evidence of interaction between silver and carbon. Despite of the change in the shape and position of the peaks, it seems that the peaks related with silver are not highly overlapped with carbon atoms. This was tested by comparing Ag and C projected states and confirmed by the calculation of other chemical and electronic parameters, as we will show in the population analysis at the end of this work. Similar behavior is observed with Ag2 and Ag3 clusters size as can be observed in Figs. S1 and S2 (Supplementary Material).

As can be observed in Fig. 4, there is again a significant change between the Ag cluster and Coronene-Epoxi isolated DOS curves compared with the Ag1/Coronene-Epoxi. However, this time there is a clear overlap between Ag states and O states peaks between –3.75 and 0 eV, which are more dispersed in energy. These results show an important contribution of the epoxy group in the interaction between Ag clusters and carbon surface. A similar behavior is observed with for Ag2 and Ag3 cluster sizes as can be observed in Figs. S3 and S4 (Supplementary Material).

The Bader charges for all the systems are shown in Table 2 . For each atom the net charge is obtained by subtracting the number of core electrons and the Bader population to the atomic number. This value represents the electron excess (negative value) or electron deficiency (positive value) by the atoms.

In Table 2, it can be observed that the average charges of the elements that compose the Agn/Coronene systems do not have high values, compatible with a low charge transfer from Agn cluster to coronene (small ΔQ values). Moreover, the net Bader charges for isolated Agn clusters are between 0.02 and 0.06 e (not reported in the Table), which are approximately the same range observed for Agn clusters adsorbed on coronene. This implies that there is no strong electronic interaction between Agn cluster and coronene C atoms, confirming the behavior observed by the DOS analysis, and the negative binding energy for the Agn/Coronene systems (see Table 1) are mainly due to van der Waals interactions. On the other hand, when the epoxy group is present, this behavior changes substantially and the charge transfer increases from 0.05 to 0.08 e to 0.09–0.57 e. The Ag atoms transfer charge to the Coronene-Epoxi substrate, particularly 0.26 and 0.57 e for Ag1 and Ag3 respectively, which in consequence leaves the Agn clusters positively charged. The transferred charge is not distributed uniformly on Coronene-Epoxi substrate. The average charge of C and H atoms remains small. In contrast, the O atoms have high negative charge values, meaning that the negative charge is concentrated on this atom. These results, evidence a strong chemical interaction between Ag and O atoms. Additionally, the charge redistribution due to these interactions could reinforce the binding between these atoms through electrostatic interactions.

A visual interpretation of the charge distribution, before and after the Ag1 adsorption on the Coronene and Coronene-Epoxi substrates would help to understand how the charge disperses along the system. With the aim to solve this issue the Bader charge values for each atom (not averages) are represented graphically with colors in Fig. 5 , following the approach taken in a previous work [61].

It can be observed in Fig. 5 that the charge is concentrated in the region where the epoxy group is located before and after the adsorption of Ag1. The other atoms, which are represented in lighter shade in the figure, are almost neutral. Before the adsorption, the O atoms concentrates a high value of negative charge (-0.87 e), which is reduced to −0.78 e after the adsorption of silver due to the charge transfer from monomer to substrate. On the other hand, in the Coronene and Ag1/Coronene systems the charge is more uniformly distributed leading to approximately neutral atoms in all the system. The explained charge distribution characteristics maintains a similar trend in the case of Ag2 and Ag3 adsorbed on Coronene and Coronene-Epoxi substrates as is shown in Fig. S5 (Supplementary Material).

In Fig. 6, an accumulation of charge in the region between the Agn clusters and O atom is observed, reflecting the bonding between Ag and the substrate for functionalized coronene through the O atom of epoxy group. This charge accumulation is also observed in the region between O and C atoms, corresponding to the bond between the two atoms. For the isovalue considered it is also noted that the
Δ ρ
graph for the silver clusters adsorbed on coronene without functional group there is not change, meaning there is not a strong interaction.

In order to obtain a better understanding of the different stages of the metal clusters adsorption on Cdots surfaces, BO and OP calculations were carried out for the clean supports, isolated silver clusters and the composite systems. The analysis is performed for Agn clusters (n = 1–3). The most relevant results for the atoms taken as reference indicated in Fig. 7 are listed in the Tables 3 and 4
. These tables also include bond distances of the most relevant bonds participating in the interaction between silver and coronene non-functionalized (Table 3) and functionalized (Table 4).

For Ag1/Coronene, the OP and BO values and bond distances of CC bond before and after the Ag adsorption are almost exactly the same, indicating a weak interaction between Ag and C atoms. The Ag2/Coronene and Ag3/Coronene systems have a similar behavior. Additionally, the Ag-Ag OP and BO values and bond distances are very similar when it is compared the isolated and adsorbed silver cluster. For all the Agn/Coronene systems there is only a variation below 2% in the OP and BO values of CC bond before and after the adsorption of silver. All these results indicate that Ag and C do not interact chemically, which is reflected in the small OP and BO values of the Ag-C bond and a bond distances higher than 3 Å, characteristic of a physical interaction. The interpretation of these results is also supported by the previous energy and charge distribution analysis.

For the Agn adsorption on Coronene-Epoxi substrate, the Ag-O OP and BO values are 0.25–0.45 and 0.34–0.65 respectively. These values are considerable higher than the ones described before for the coronene substrate without epoxy group, where the interaction occurs directly between C and Ag. There is consistency between this analysis and the charge study performed previously. The comparison between Ag-O and Ag-C OP and BO values shows an increment between 422 and 669 % and 490 and 820 % respectively, demonstrating the importance for Cdots surfaces to be functionalized for the interaction with silver. For that reason, the BO and OP values of CO bond before and after the silver adsorption change between 6 and 17 %. However, the CO bonds OP and BO values are still high after the silver clusters adsorption, denoting that despite the fact of the strong interaction between the Agn clusters and O atom, the formed complex remains stable, bound to coronene through the CO bond of the epoxy group. This fact is also evidenced by the calculated bond distances.

The adsorption of silver clusters on Cdots surfaces is investigated employing a coronene model through DFT calculations, studying the stability, bond distances, DOS and PDOS curves and charge distribution. Moreover, a bonding analysis is also included to complement the electronic structure analysis. A comparison between Agn clusters (n = 1–3) on coronene without and with epoxy group is performed. From this study, the importance of the role of oxygenated functional groups to anchor silver clusters on Cdots surfaces, is revealed. The absence of oxidized carbon on Cdot surfaces leads to a very weak interaction, between the carbon surface and the Agn clusters, that is dominated by physical forces. Charge transfer and orbital overlap occur between Ag and O atoms allowing the adsorption of silver clusters in a stable way on functionalized carbon surfaces. Furthermore, the epoxy group can remain stable after these Ag-O relative strong interactions with overlapped states between C and O atoms and conserving the strength of their bond, which leads to the thermodynamic stability of the Ag@Cdot composite material.

Y. Robein: Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. R.E. Ambrusi: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision. M.E. Pronsato: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Visualization. M.S. Di Nezio: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. G. Brizuela: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank to ANPCyT (PICT-2016-4094). R.E. Ambrusi, M.E. Pronsato, and G. Brizuela are members of CONICET. G. Brizuela acknowledges SGCyT, UNS, Argentina (PGI 24/F078). M.S. Di Nezio acknowledges SGCyT, UNS, Argentina (PGI 24/Q110) and INQUISUR (UNS-CONICET).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.comptc.2023.114087.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticles as highly efficient and selective optical probe for sulphide via dendrimer formation in aqueous medium †

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Sanskriti, K.K. Upadhyay, Silver nanoparticles as highly efficient and selective optical probe for sulphide via dendrimer formation in aqueous medium †, RSC Adv. 6 (2016) 14563-14569. 10.1039/c5ra25625a.
